html:
[201]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 猥亵之语 - Refers to obscene or vulgar language.
  • 达君听 - Means to reach the ears of the king or the emperor, indicating the act of conveying information to a ruler.
  • 假惺惺 - Means 'fake show of sympathy' or 'pretending to be kind.'
  • 作态 - Means 'to put on an act' or 'to behave artificially.'
  • 遍播扬 - Means 'to spread everywhere' or 'to publicize widely.'
  • 情见于色 - Means 'to show one's feelings on one's face.'
  • 默不一语 - Means 'to remain silent without speaking a word.'
  • 身首异处 - Means 'to be separated from the body,' typically referring to decapitation.
  • 戛然有声 - Means 'to make a sharp sound.'
  • 灿若长虹 - Means 'to shine like a rainbow.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 君 - Refers to the king or emperor, indicating a hierarchical relationship.
  • 少年 - Refers to a young man, often used in classical texts to denote a character with a specific role or personality.
  • 贞洁人 - Refers to a virtuous or chaste person, reflecting traditional values of morality and purity.
  • 白狐 - In Chinese folklore, white foxes are often associated with supernatural beings or spirits, sometimes with magical abilities.

Proper Nouns

  • 君 - The king or emperor.
  • 少年 - A young man.
  • 白狐 - A white fox.

[202]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大骇 - Means 'greatly frightened' or 'shocked,' expressing a strong emotional reaction.
  • 此君之娈童也 - Refers to 'this is the master's young male lover,' indicating a relationship between an adult and a younger male.
  • 我固恕之，奈渠定不欲生何 - Means 'I certainly forgive him, but what can I do about his unwillingness to live?' Reflects the speaker's internal conflict between forgiveness and the victim's despair.
  • 收刃入囊 - Literally 'sheathed the blade back into the bag,' indicating the action of putting away a weapon.
  • 曳令入 - Means 'dragged him in,' describing a forceful action.
  • 适妖物败意 - Means 'just now, the demon ruined our plans,' referring to a supernatural event that disrupted their intentions.
  • 请俟来宵 - Means 'please wait until tomorrow night,' suggesting a postponement of their plans.
  • 遂共绸缪 - Means 'they then engaged in intimate relations,' describing a physical encounter.
  • 诘其术 - Means 'asked about the method,' inquiring into a specific skill or technique.
  • 此非君所知 - Means 'this is not something you can understand,' indicating that the knowledge is beyond the listener's comprehension.
  • 宜须慎秘，泄恐不为君福 - Means 'it should be kept secret and handled with care, as revealing it might not be good for you,' emphasizing the importance of confidentiality.
  • 又订以嫁娶 - Means 'they also agreed on marriage,' suggesting a formal commitment.
  • 枕席焉，提汲焉 - Means 'for sleeping and waking, for carrying water and fetching firewood,' referring to the practical aspects of married life.
  • 非妇伊何也 - Means 'who else could it be but a wife?' rhetorically affirming the role of a wife.
  • 业夫妇矣，何必复言嫁娶乎 - Means 'we are already husband and wife, so why speak of marriage again?' indicating that the formalities of marriage are already completed.
  • 将勿憎吾贫耶 - Means 'will you not be disgusted by my poverty?' expressing concern about the other's feelings regarding the speaker's financial situation.
  • 君固贫，妾富耶 - Means 'you are poor, and I am wealthy?' rhetorically questioning the disparity in their financial status.
  • 今宵之聚，正以怜君贫耳 - Means 'our meeting tonight is precisely because I pity your poverty,' explaining the reason for their encounter.
  • 临别嘱曰 - Means 'at parting, she said,' introducing a final piece of advice or warning.
  • 苟且之行，不可以屡 - Means 'casual or improper actions should not be repeated,' advising against repeated indiscretions.
  • 当来我自来，不当来相强无益 - Means 'when it is time, I will come on my own; if it is not time, forcing it will be of no benefit,' suggesting that timing is crucial.
  • 后相值，每欲引与私语，女辄走避 - Means 'afterward, whenever they met, she would try to lead him to private conversation, but the girl would always avoid it,' indicating a pattern of behavior.
  • 然衣绽炊薪，悉为纪理，不啻妇也 - Means 'yet she handled everything, from sewing clothes to cooking, as if she were a wife,' describing the girl's domestic responsibilities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 娈童 - Refers to a young male lover, a common theme in ancient Chinese literature and culture.
  • 妖物 - Refers to a supernatural entity, often a demon or spirit, which can cause harm or disruption.
  • 绸缪 - A term often used to describe intimate relations, sometimes with a connotation of marital fidelity.
  • 嫁娶 - Refers to the formal process of marriage, including both the ceremony and the legal aspects.
  • 枕席 - Refers to the bed, symbolizing the intimate and private aspects of married life.
  • 提汲 - Refers to carrying water and fetching firewood, traditional household chores that symbolize the practical aspects of married life.
  • 妇 - Refers to a wife, emphasizing the role and responsibilities of a married woman.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces a strong emotional reaction followed by a direct statement from the female character.
  • This sentence expresses a conflict between forgiveness and the victim's despair.
  • This sequence describes a series of actions and a request for postponement.
  • This sequence describes an intimate encounter followed by a question and a response.
  • This sequence emphasizes the importance of secrecy and the practical aspects of marriage.
  • This rhetorical question affirms the completion of the marriage.
  • This rhetorical question expresses concern about the other's feelings regarding poverty.
  • This rhetorical question questions the disparity in their financial status.
  • This sentence explains the reason for their encounter.
  • This sentence provides final advice and a warning.
  • This sequence describes a pattern of behavior and the girl's domestic responsibilities.

[203]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 積數月 - Literally 'accumulate for several months,' meaning 'after several months.'
  • 生竭力葬之 - '生' (the son) '竭力' (exerted all his strength) '葬之' (to bury her), meaning the son buried his mother with great effort.
  • 意孤寝可乱 - '意' (intends) '孤寝' (to sleep alone) '可乱' (can be misinterpreted), meaning the son thought that sleeping alone could lead to misunderstandings.
  • 逾垣入 - '逾' (climb over) '垣' (wall) '入' (enter), meaning the son climbed over the wall to enter.
  • 隔窗频呼 - '隔窗' (through the window) '频呼' (frequently calling), meaning he called out frequently through the window.
  • 迄不应 - '迄' (until) '不应' (no response), meaning there was no response.
  • 视其门，则空室扁焉 - '视其门' (look at the door) '则' (then) '空室扁焉' (the room was empty and the sign was hanging), meaning when he looked at the door, the room was empty and the sign was hanging.
  • 窃疑女有他约 - '窃' (secretly) '疑' (suspected) '女有他约' (the woman had another appointment), meaning he secretly suspected the woman had another engagement.
  • 夜复往 - '夜' (at night) '复' (again) '往' (went), meaning he went again at night.
  • 遂留佩玉于窗间而去之 - '遂' (then) '留' (left) '佩玉' (a jade pendant) '于窗间' (on the window) '而去之' (and left), meaning he then left a jade pendant on the window and left.
  • 越日 - '越' (the next day) '日' (day), meaning the next day.
  • 相遇于母所 - '相遇' (met) '于母所' (at the mother's place), meaning they met at the mother's place.
  • 女尾其后 - '女' (the woman) '尾' (followed) '其后' (behind him), meaning the woman followed behind him.
  • 君疑妾耶 - '君' (you) '疑' (doubt) '妾' (I) '耶' (yes), meaning do you doubt me?
  • 人各有心，不可以告人 - '人各有心' (everyone has their own mind) '不可以告人' (cannot be told to others), meaning everyone has their own thoughts and cannot be revealed to others.
  • 妾身未分明 - '妾身' (myself) '未分明' (not clearly defined), meaning my status is not clearly defined.
  • 能为君生之，不能为君育之 - '能' (can) '为君' (for you) '生之' (give birth to it) '不能' (cannot) '育之' (raise it), meaning I can give it birth for you but cannot raise it for you.
  • 可密告母觅乳媪 - '可' (can) '密告' (secretly tell) '母' (mother) '觅' (seek) '乳媪' (nurse), meaning you can secretly tell your mother to seek a wet nurse.
  • 伪为讨螟蛉者 - '伪' (pretend) '为' (to) '讨' (adopt) '螟蛉' (foster child), meaning pretend to adopt a foster child.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 母死 - Refers to the death of the mother.
  • 竭力葬之 - Reflects the traditional Chinese practice of burying one's parents with great effort.
  • 孤寝可乱 - Implies the cultural concern about the potential for misunderstandings or inappropriate behavior when someone is alone.
  • 逾垣入 - Indicates the act of entering a place by climbing over a wall, which might be seen as a breach of propriety.
  • 隔窗频呼 - Suggests a traditional method of communication in ancient times, often used in courting or seeking help.
  • 视其门，则空室扁焉 - Implies the absence of the woman, possibly indicating that she had left or was not there.
  • 窃疑女有他约 - Reflects the cultural suspicion and jealousy in relationships.
  • 夜复往 - Indicates the persistence and determination of the son.
  • 留佩玉于窗间而去之 - The act of leaving a jade pendant is a symbol of affection and commitment in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 越日 - Refers to the next day, a common way to denote time in classical Chinese.
  • 相遇于母所 - Indicates a meeting at the mother's place, which might be a neutral or significant location.
  • 女尾其后 - Suggests the woman's following behavior, which could imply her feelings or intentions.
  • 君疑妾耶 - A direct question to address the son's doubts.
  • 人各有心，不可以告人 - Reflects the idea that people have their own thoughts and feelings that they keep private.
  • 妾身未分明 - Implies the woman's uncertain status, possibly due to her pregnancy.
  • 能为君生之，不能为君育之 - Reflects the traditional division of roles in child-rearing, where the woman can give birth but not raise the child.
  • 可密告母觅乳媪 - Suggests a practical solution to the problem of raising the child.
  • 伪为讨螟蛉者 - Refers to the practice of adopting a foster child, which was sometimes used to avoid social stigma or legal complications.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 积数月，其母死，生竭力葬之 - This sequence of events must be read as a chronological progression.
  • 生意孤寝可乱，逾垣入，隔窗频呼，迄不应 - This sequence describes the son's actions and their outcomes, which must be read in order.
  • 视其门，则空室扁焉 - This clause must be read as a result of the previous action.
  • 窃疑女有他约 - This clause introduces the son's suspicion, which must be read as a reaction to the previous events.
  • 夜复往，亦如之 - This clause indicates the repetition of the son's actions, which must be read as a continuation of the previous sequence.
  • 遂留佩玉于窗间而去之 - This clause describes the son's final action, which must be read as a conclusion to the previous sequence.
  • 越日，相遇于母所 - This clause introduces a new event, which must be read as a transition to a new setting.
  • 女尾其后曰 - This clause introduces the woman's response, which must be read as a reaction to the son's actions.
  • 君疑妾耶 - This question must be read as a direct address to the son.
  • 人各有心，不可以告人 - This clause must be read as an explanation of the woman's feelings.
  • 今欲使君无疑，乌得可 - This clause must be read as the woman's intention to clear the son's doubts.
  • 然一事烦急为谋 - This clause introduces the woman's request, which must be read as a continuation of her explanation.
  • 问之，曰 - This clause must be read as the son's response to the woman's request.
  • 妾体孕已八月矣，恐旦晚临盆 - This clause describes the woman's condition, which must be read as the reason for her request.
  • 妾身未分明，能为君生之，不能为君育之 - This clause explains the woman's limitations, which must be read as a continuation of her explanation.
  • 可密告母觅乳媪，伪为讨螟蛉者，勿言妾也 - This clause provides the woman's solution, which must be read as a conclusion to her explanation.

[204]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生诺 - A polite way of saying 'yes' or 'I agree,' often used in formal or respectful contexts.
  • 聘之不可 - Means 'cannot be engaged in marriage,' referring to the inability to arrange a marriage.
  • 顾私于我儿 - Literally 'consider private matters with my son,' implying a personal or private arrangement.
  • 又月余 - Means 'another month or more,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 萧萧闭寂 - Describes a scene of silence and desolation, literally 'silent and deserted.'
  • 呱呱者 - Refers to a baby crying, literally 'crying sound.'
  • 区区隐衷 - Means 'a small, hidden heart,' referring to a private or sensitive matter.
  • 区区 - Can mean 'small' or 'modest,' often used to express humility.
  • 隐衷 - Refers to a hidden or private matter.
  • 捉绷席而视之 - Means 'to take the cloth and look at it,' referring to the act of examining something.
  • 丰颐而广额 - Describes a face with a full cheek and broad forehead, indicating a handsome or well-proportioned appearance.
  • 伶仃一身 - Means 'alone and destitute,' describing someone who is solitary and in need of support.
  • 将焉所托 - Means 'where to entrust or rely on,' indicating a need for support or care.
  • 俟夜无人 - Means 'wait until night when there is no one around,' suggesting a private or secretive arrangement.
  • 窃共异之 - Means 'secretly find it strange,' indicating a sense of surprise or curiosity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 聘之不可 - Refers to the traditional practice of arranged marriages in Chinese culture, where marriage was often arranged by families.
  • 母笑曰 - The mother's laughter suggests a sense of amusement or relief, possibly at the situation's irony or the daughter's unexpected behavior.
  • 区区隐衷 - The phrase reflects the cultural emphasis on modesty and the concealment of personal feelings or secrets.
  • 俟夜无人 - The suggestion to wait until night when no one is around reflects a cultural context where private matters were often discussed in secrecy.

[205]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 更数夕，夜将半 - Indicates the passage of several nights, approaching midnight.
  • 养母之德，刻刻不去诸怀 - Figurative expression meaning 'the virtue of raising a mother is constantly in my heart.'
  • 可一而不可再 - Proverbial phrase meaning 'can be done once but not twice,' emphasizing the uniqueness of an opportunity or action.
  • 为君贫不能婚，将为君延一线之续 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'because you are poor and cannot marry, I will extend a thread of hope for you.'
  • 一索而得 - Figurative expression meaning 'to find something easily or quickly.'
  • 信水复来 - Metaphorical expression referring to a recurrence of difficult circumstances.
  • 破戒而再 - Phrase meaning 'to break a vow or rule and do it again,' often used in a religious or moral context.
  • 囊中何物 - Direct question meaning 'What is in the bag?'
  • 仇人头耳 - Literal description meaning 'the head of an enemy.'
  • 向不与君言者，以机事不密，惧有宣泄 - Figurative expression meaning 'I did not tell you before because the matter was secret and I feared it would be revealed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 养母 - Refers to the act of raising a mother, emphasizing filial piety and maternal care.
  • 床第 - Euphemism for sexual relations or the marital bed.
  • 浙人 - Refers to someone from Zhejiang, a province in southeastern China.
  • 司马 - Ancient Chinese military rank, often translated as 'marshal' or 'general.'
  • 陷于仇 - Refers to being captured or betrayed by an enemy.
  • 籍吾家 - Phrase meaning 'to enslave or register my family,' indicating a severe form of punishment or subjugation.

[206]

Proper Nouns

  • 異史氏 - A title used by Pu Songling in his *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* to introduce a commentary or moral.
  • 舉進士 - A term referring to passing the imperial examination to become a Jinshi (advanced scholar).
  • 奉祖母以終老 - A term referring to honoring one's grandmother until the end of one's life.

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 耽飲 - Literally means 'to indulge in drinking,' indicating a strong fondness or addiction to alcohol.
  • 浮三白 - Refers to a custom of drinking three cups of wine before going to bed, often associated with a specific ritual or practice.
  • 床頭樽常不空 - Literally means 'the wine jar by the bed is never empty,' indicating a constant supply of wine.
  • 似有人共臥者 - Literally means 'it seems like someone is sharing the bed,' suggesting an unusual presence.
  • 覆裳堕耳 - Literally means 'the coverlet has fallen,' implying that the sensation was due to the coverlet falling off.
  • 似猫而巨 - Literally means 'like a cat but large,' describing the size and appearance of the creature.
  • 酣醉而大卧 - Literally means 'deeply drunk and lying down,' indicating the state of the creature.
  • 此我酒友也 - Literally means 'this is my drinking companion,' expressing the narrator's recognition of the creature.
  • 覆衣加臂 - Literally means 'covering the body with a garment,' indicating a protective action.
  • 與之共寢 - Literally means 'sleeping with it,' showing the narrator's willingness to share the bed.
  • 留燭以觀其變 - Literally means 'leaving the candle to observe its changes,' indicating the narrator's curiosity.
  • 半夜狐欠伸 - Literally means 'in the middle of the night, the fox stretches,' describing the fox's movement.
  • 美哉睡乎 - Literally means 'what a beautiful sleep,' expressing the narrator's admiration.
  • 啟覆視之 - Literally means 'opening the cover to look at it,' indicating the narrator's action to see the creature.
  • 儒冠之俊人也 - Literally means 'a handsome man with a scholar's cap,' describing the fox's appearance.
  • 起拜榻前 - Literally means 'rising to bow before the bed,' indicating the fox's respectful gesture.
  • 謝不殺之恩 - Literally means 'thanking for the grace of not being killed,' expressing gratitude.
  • 我癖於曲蘖 - Literally means 'I am addicted to fermented grains,' indicating the narrator's fondness for alcohol.
  • 卿，我鲍叔也 - Literally means 'you are my Bo Shu,' referring to a trusted friend, similar to the historical figure Bo Shu.
  • 如不见疑 - Literally means 'if you do not doubt me,' expressing a request for trust.
  • 当为糟丘之良友 - Literally means 'you will be a good friend of the wine hill,' indicating a promise of friendship.
  • 曳登榻复寝 - Literally means 'pulling [the fox] onto the bed to sleep again,' showing the narrator's continued hospitality.
  • 无相猜 - Literally means 'do not suspect each other,' expressing a desire for mutual trust.
  • 生既醒，则狐已去 - Literally means 'when the narrator woke up, the fox had already left,' indicating the fox's departure.
  • 乃治旨酒一盛专伺狐 - Literally means 'then he prepared a special wine to wait for the fox,' showing the narrator's anticipation of the fox's return.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 浮三白 - A traditional Chinese custom where three cups of wine are drunk before going to bed, often associated with a specific ritual or practice.
  • 儒冠 - A scholar's cap, symbolizing education and refinement.
  • 鲍叔 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and trustworthiness, often used as a metaphor for a trusted friend.
  • 糟丘 - A metaphor for a place of excessive drinking, often used to describe a tavern or a place where people gather to drink.

Proper Nouns

  • 車生者 - A character name, likely 'Che Sheng.'
  • 浮三白 - A traditional custom of drinking three cups of wine before going to bed.
  • 儒冠 - A scholar's cap.
  • 鲍叔 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and trustworthiness.
  • 糟丘 - A metaphor for a place of excessive drinking.

[208]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 抵夕果至 - Arrived by evening, indicating the person arrived exactly as expected.
  • 促膝歡飲 - Sit close together and drink merrily, describing an intimate and joyful drinking session.
  • 狐量豪善諧 - Generous, skilled, and witty, highlighting the fox's positive qualities.
  • 恨相得晚 - Regret meeting so late, expressing a sense of belated appreciation or friendship.
  • 叨良醞 - Receive good wine, used to express gratitude for a favor.
  • 斗酒之歡 - Joy of a shared drink, emphasizing the pleasure of companionship.
  • 杖頭錢大不易 - Money on the walking stick is not easy to come by, referring to the difficulty of making a living.
  • 當為君少謀酒資 - I will help you with some money for drinks, showing the fox's generosity.
  • 去此東南七里道側有遺金 - There is some lost money seven miles southeast of here, indicating a specific location.
  • 果得二金 - Indeed found two gold pieces, confirming the success of the fox's advice.
  • 市佳肴 - Buy good dishes, indicating the purchase of fine food.
  • 院後有窖藏宜發之 - There is a hidden treasure in the courtyard that should be unearthed, suggesting a valuable discovery.
  • 錢百余千 - Over a hundred thousand coins, indicating a significant amount of money.
  • 囊中已自有，莫漫愁沽矣 - You already have enough in your bag, so don't worry about buying more, advising against unnecessary worry.
  • 不然。轍中水胡可以久掬 - Not so. Water in the rut can't be scooped up forever, suggesting that the current situation is temporary.
  • 合更謀之 - We should think of something else, indicating the need for further planning.
  • 市上荞價廉 - The price of buckwheat in the market is low, indicating a good investment opportunity.
  • 奇貨可居 - A rare commodity is worth holding, suggesting that the buckwheat is a valuable investment.
  • 收荞四十余石 - Collected over forty dan of buckwheat, indicating a large quantity.
  • 人咸非笑之 - People all criticized and laughed at him, showing the initial negative reaction to the action.
  • 未幾大旱 - Soon after, a great drought occurred, indicating a significant event.
  • 禾豆盡枯 - All the grains and beans withered, describing the impact of the drought.
  • 惟荞可種 - Only buckwheat could be planted, highlighting the unique situation.
  • 售種息十倍 - Selling the seeds yielded ten times the profit, indicating a significant financial gain.
  • 由此益富 - Thus becoming even richer, showing the result of the investment.
  • 治沃田二百畝 - Managed two hundred acres of fertile land, indicating the expansion of resources.
  • 多種麥則麥收，多種黍則黍收 - Plant more wheat, and you get more wheat; plant more millet, and you get more millet, showing the principle of cause and effect.
  • 一切種植之早晚皆取決於狐 - All the timing of planting was decided by the fox, indicating the fox's influence.
  • 日稔密 - Became very close over time, describing the deepening relationship.
  • 呼生妻以嫂 - Called the man's wife 'sister-in-law,' showing a familial relationship.
  • 視子猶子焉 - Treated his son like his own, indicating the fox's affection.
  • 後生卒，狐遂不復來 - After the man died, the fox no longer came, indicating the end of the relationship.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - In Chinese folklore, foxes are often associated with trickery and magic, but here the fox is portrayed positively as a helpful and generous spirit.
  • 杖頭錢 - Refers to the small amount of money carried on a walking stick, symbolizing the difficulty of making a living.
  • 窖藏 - Refers to hidden treasures, often associated with ancient Chinese legends and stories of wealth.
  • 大旱 - Refers to a severe drought, a common natural disaster in Chinese history.
  • 禾豆 - Refers to grains and beans, essential crops in ancient Chinese agriculture.
  • 荞 - Refers to buckwheat, a crop that is less commonly grown but can be more resilient in certain conditions.
  • 沃田 - Refers to fertile land, a valuable resource in ancient Chinese society.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sequence sets the scene for the arrival and the intimate drinking session.
  • The fox's qualities are introduced, followed by the man's regret at meeting him later.
  • The fox's advice is given, and the man follows it successfully.
  • The fox's next advice leads to a significant discovery.
  • The man's investment in buckwheat yields a substantial profit, leading to increased wealth.
  • The fox's influence over the man's agricultural decisions is emphasized.
  • The man's death marks the end of the fox's involvement in his life.

[209]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 莲香 - Metaphorical or symbolic name, possibly referring to a beautiful or fragrant lotus flower, symbolizing purity and beauty.
  • 倾国之姝 - Term used to describe a woman of extraordinary beauty, often found in classical Chinese literature.
  • The phrase '丈夫何畏鬼狐' is a rhetorical question meaning 'what does a man fear from ghosts and foxes?' It reflects a humorous and exaggerated statement about bravery.
  • The phrase '雄来吾有利剑，雌者尚当开门纳之' is a humorous and exaggerated statement meaning 'if a male ghost comes, I have a sharp sword; if a female ghost comes, I will still open the door for her.' It adds a layer of humor and bravado to the narrative.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 沂州 - A place name in China, known for its historical and cultural significance.
  • 红花埠 - A specific location in Yizhou, possibly a small town or village.
  • 鬼狐 - Refers to ghosts and fox spirits, common figures in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 妓女 - Refers to a courtesan or prostitute, a common figure in classical Chinese literature.

[210]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夕獨坐凝思 - One night, sitting alone in deep thought, setting a reflective mood.
  • 翩然入 - Describes the graceful and sudden entrance of the woman, emphasizing her ethereal appearance.

  • 兄屡止之，终不听 - His brother repeatedly tried to stop him, but he finally did not listen; '屡' (lv) means 'repeatedly,' '止' (zhi) means 'stopped,' '终' (zhong) means 'finally,' and '不听' (bu ting) means 'did not listen.'

  • 然亦奇矣 - Means 'yet it is still remarkable.' Expresses surprise or admiration despite the previous criticism.
  • 在都偶过市廛 - Means 'in the capital, he happened to pass by a market.' Sets the scene and provides context for the event.
  • 闻弦歌声，观者如堵 - Means 'he heard the sound of stringed instruments and singing, and the audience was like a wall.' Vividly conveys the popularity and attention the performance attracted.
  • 近窥之，则见一少年曼声度曲 - Means 'upon closer inspection, he saw a young man singing softly and gracefully.' Describes the performer and his manner of singing.
  • 并无乐器，惟以一指捺颊际 - Means 'there were no musical instruments; he only used one finger to press against his cheek.' Highlights the unique method used by the performer.

  • 亦口技之苗裔也 - Means 'it is also a descendant of the mouth technique.' Connects the modern performance to the traditional art form.

  • 由此夜夜必至 - Indicates that, from this point on, the person would visit every night.
  • 每与阖户雅饮 - Every time they would close the door and have a refined drinking session together.
  • 谈及音律，辄能剖悉宫商 - Whenever they talked about music theory, she could always analyze and explain the five tones (宫, 商, 角, 征, 羽).
  • 公遂意其工于度曲 - Thus thought that she was skilled in composing or arranging music.
  • 儿时之所习也 - What was practiced or learned in childhood.
  • 卿勿为亡国之音，使人悒悒 - Do not play music associated with the fall of a nation, for it makes people feel sad or depressed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 音律 - Refers to the principles of music in Chinese tradition, including the five tones (宫, 商, 角, 征, 羽) and the twelve tones (十二律).
  • 宫商 - Specifically refers to the palatal (宫) and nasal (商) tones in traditional Chinese music.
  • 亡国之音 - A reference to the idea that certain types of music, especially those associated with lamentation or mourning, can evoke feelings of sadness or despair, often linked to the decline or fall of a nation.
  • 悒悒 - A term describing a state of sadness or depression, often used in classical Chinese literature to convey emotional distress.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '由此夜夜必至，每与阖户雅饮' is elliptical but can be read as 'From this point on, she would visit every night, and they would close the door and have a refined drinking session together.'
  • The phrase '谈及音律，辄能剖悉宫商' is also elliptical but can be read as 'Whenever they talked about music theory, she could always analyze and explain the five tones (宫, 商, 角, 征, 羽).'
  • The phrase '卿勿为亡国之音，使人悒悒' is a direct address but can be read as 'Do not play music associated with the fall of a nation, for it makes people feel sad or depressed.'

  • 受君恩義，数世不足以酬 - '受君恩義' means 'received your kindness and righteousness,' '数世' means 'several generations,' and '不足以酬' means 'insufficient to repay.' This phrase means 'received your kindness and righteousness, several generations are insufficient to repay.'
  • 蒙惠及泉下人 - '蒙惠' means 'received your favor,' '及' means 'to,' and '泉下人' means 'the dead.' This phrase means 'received your favor to the dead.'
  • 经咒藏满 - '经咒' means 'scriptures and mantras,' and '藏满' means 'full.' This phrase means 'scriptures and mantras are full.'
  • 今得生河北卢户部家 - '今' means 'now,' '得生' means 'reincarnated,' '河北' means 'Hebei,' and '卢户部家' means 'Lu's household.' This phrase means 'now reincarnated in Lu's household in Hebei.'
  • 如不忘今日，过此十五年，八月十六日，烦一往会 - '如不忘今日' means 'if you do not forget today,' '过此十五年' means 'after fifteen years,' '八月十六日' means 'August 16th,' and '烦一往会' means 'trouble yourself to come.' This phrase means 'if you do not forget today, after fifteen years, on August 16th, trouble yourself to come.'
  • 生三十余年矣，又十五年，将就木焉 - '生三十余年矣' means 'I have been alive for over thirty years,' '又十五年' means 'another fifteen years,' and '将就木焉' means 'will be in the coffin.' This phrase means 'I have been alive for over thirty years, another fifteen years, I will be in the coffin.'
  • 愿为奴婢以报 - '愿为' means 'willing to be,' '奴婢' means 'servant,' and '以报' means 'to repay.' This phrase means 'willing to be a servant to repay.'
  • 少间曰 - '少间' means 'after a while,' and '曰' means 'said.' This phrase means 'after a while, she said.'
  • 尽此，且去 - '尽此' means 'up to this point,' and '且去' means 'and leave.' This phrase means 'up to this point, and leave.'
  • 勿忘所言 - '勿忘' means 'do not forget,' and '所言' means 'what I said.' This phrase means 'do not forget what I said.'
  • 展軨即发 - '展軨' means 'the carriage stretched out,' and '即发' means 'immediately departed.' This phrase means 'the carriage immediately departed.'
  • 车马阗咽而去 - '车马' means 'carriages and horses,' '阗咽' means 'crowded,' and '而去' means 'and left.' This phrase means 'carriages and horses crowded and left.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 河北 - Historically, the region north of the Yellow River was significant in Chinese politics and culture, often associated with military and administrative centers.
  • 户部 - The Ministry of Revenue was one of the most important government departments, responsible for managing the national treasury and financial affairs.
  • 生而能言 - The ability to speak from birth is often seen as a sign of exceptional intelligence or divine favor in Chinese literature.
  • 贵家委禽 - The traditional practice of offering a pair of birds as a betrothal gift is a cultural reference to formal marriage proposals.
  • 痴婢 - The term '痴婢' is used here in a teasing manner, reflecting the playful and sometimes condescending attitude towards young women in classical literature.
  • 半百 - The term '半百' is a poetic way of saying 'fifty years old,' often used to describe someone of advanced age.
  • 发童而齿壑 - The poetic expression '发童而齿壑' vividly describes the signs of old age, emphasizing the physical changes that come with time.
  • 戒阍人勿通客 - The act of instructing the gatekeeper not to admit visitors is a common strategy used to avoid unwanted guests or to maintain privacy.
  • 过期以绝其望 - The tactic of passing the deadline to cut off hopes is a strategic move often used in negotiations or to discourage further pursuit.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '未几夫人以老病卒' is elliptical and can be read as 'shortly after, the wife died of old age and illness.'
  • The phrase '子欲为求继室于朱门' is elliptical and can be read as 'the son wanted to seek a second wife from a wealthy family.'
  • The phrase '生曰：“待吾至河北来而后娶。”' is elliptical and can be read as 'the husband said, 'Wait until I come back from Hebei before we get married.''
  • The phrase '屈指已及约期，遂命仆马至河北' is elliptical and can be read as 'counting on his fingers, he found that the agreed-upon date had arrived, so he ordered his servants and horses to go to Hebei.'
  • The phrase '访之，果有卢户部' is elliptical and can be read as 'he went to look for him, and indeed, there was Lu, the Minister of Revenue.'
  • The phrase '先是，卢公生一女，生而能言，长益慧美，父母最钟爱之' is elliptical and can be read as 'previously, Minister Lu had a daughter who could speak from birth, grew even more intelligent and beautiful, and was deeply loved by her parents.'
  • The phrase '贵家委禽，女辄不欲，怪问之，具述生前约' is elliptical and can be read as 'when wealthy families proposed marriage, the girl refused, and when questioned, she explained the premarital agreement.'
  • The phrase '共计其年，大笑曰：“痴婢！张郎计今年已半百，人事变迁，其骨已朽。纵其尚在，发童而齿壑矣。”' is elliptical and can be read as 'calculating the years, she laughed and said, 'Foolish maid! Zhang Lang is now fifty years old, human affairs change, his bones have rotted. Even if he is still alive, his hair is gone and his teeth are loose.''
  • The phrase '女不听' is elliptical and can be read as 'the girl refused to listen.'
  • The phrase '母见其志不摇，与卢公谋，戒阍人勿通客，过期以绝其望' is elliptical and can be read as 'the mother, seeing that her daughter's resolve did not waver, consulted with Minister Lu, instructed the gatekeeper not to admit visitors, and passed the deadline to cut off his hopes.'
  • The phrase '未几生至，阍人拒之，退返旅舍，怅恨无所为计' is elliptical and can be read as 'shortly after, the husband arrived, but the gatekeeper refused to admit him, so he returned to the inn, feeling dejected and having no plan.'
  • The phrase '闲游郊郭，因循而暗访之' is elliptical and can be read as 'he leisurely strolled around the outskirts of the city, following along and secretly investigating.'

  • 急視徐，徐尚未醒 - Quickly looked at Xu, who was still not awake.
  • 見其枕遺屙之石，酣寢敗廁中 - Saw that he was using a stone for excrement, deeply sleeping in the ruined toilet.
  • 蹴起，互相駭異 - Kicked him up, and they were both startled.
  • 四顧，則一庭荒草，兩間破屋而已 - Looked around, and it was just a courtyard full of weeds and two ruined houses.

  • This sequence must be read as a single unit, where the new wife met her in-laws, who were unusually warm and beautiful, and the host was very happy.
  • This sequence must be read as a single unit, where the groom and his younger brother came to see her off, their speech was all elegant, and they were also good at drinking.
  • This phrase must be read as a conclusion to the previous sequence, where they left at dawn.
  • This sequence must be read as a single unit, where the new wife could also predict whether the year would be a good or bad harvest, therefore, all plans for livelihood took her as a model.

  • 彼黑松林戎马顿来，固相安矣 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'those black pines and warhorses suddenly arrive, naturally settling into peace,' referring to the unexpected arrival of forces that bring stability.
  • 设黄龙府潮水忽至，何以御之 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'if the yellow dragon city's tide suddenly arrives, how can it be defended against,' referring to the unpredictability and difficulty of defending against sudden threats.
  • 宜断其钻刺之恨，兼塞其送迎之路 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'it is appropriate to cut off the hatred of drilling and piercing, and to block the road of sending and receiving,' referring to the need to resolve conflicts and prevent further entanglements.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 笑判 - A term used in classical Chinese literature to describe a humorous or satirical judgment, often used in legal or moral contexts.
  • 男女居室，为夫妇之大伦 - Refers to the fundamental principle of marriage in Confucian thought, emphasizing the importance of the marital relationship.
  • 燥湿互通，乃阴阳之正窍 - Refers to the balance of yin and yang, a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy and medicine.
  • 迎风待月，尚有荡检之讥 - Refers to the criticism of behavior that is overly casual or improper, often associated with the Song Dynasty's moral codes.
  • 断袖分桃，难免掩鼻之丑 - Refers to inappropriate or scandalous behavior, often associated with the Han Dynasty's moral codes.
  • 人必力士，鸟道乃敢生开 - Refers to the need for strength and courage to undertake difficult tasks, often associated with the Tang Dynasty's military ethos.
  • 洞非桃源，渔篙宁许误人 - Refers to the idea that if the situation is not ideal, it cannot lead to positive outcomes, often associated with the Song Dynasty's philosophical thought.
  • 云雨未兴，辄尔上下其手 - Refers to premature or inappropriate actions, often associated with the Ming Dynasty's moral codes.
  • 阴阳反背，居然表里为奸 - Refers to the corruption of moral principles, often associated with the Qing Dynasty's moral codes.
  • 华池置无用之乡，谬说老僧入定 - Refers to the misplacement of resources or talents, often associated with the Tang Dynasty's philosophical thought.
  • 蛮洞乃不毛之地，遂使眇帅称戈 - Refers to the futility of fighting in a hopeless situation, often associated with the Song Dynasty's military ethos.
  • 系赤兔于辕门，如将射戟 - Refers to a show of force or threat, often associated with the Han Dynasty's military ethos.
  • 探大弓于国库，直欲斩关 - Refers to a bold or reckless action, often associated with the Tang Dynasty's military ethos.
  • 或是监内黄鳣，访知交于昨夜 - Refers to an unlikely or improbable situation, often associated with the Song Dynasty's philosophical thought.
  • 分明王家朱李，索钻报于来生 - Refers to a long-standing grudge or unresolved conflict, often associated with the Ming Dynasty's moral codes.
  • 彼黑松林戎马顿来，固相安矣 - Refers to the unexpected arrival of forces that bring stability, often associated with the Qing Dynasty's military ethos.
  • 设黄龙府潮水忽至，何以御之 - Refers to the unpredictability and difficulty of defending against sudden threats, often associated with the Tang Dynasty's military ethos.
  • 宜断其钻刺之恨，兼塞其送迎之路 - Refers to the need to resolve conflicts and prevent further entanglements, often associated with the Song Dynasty's moral codes.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 睨之 - Means 'glanced at' or 'looked sideways,' suggesting a casual or curious glance.
  • 甚哀 - Means 'very sad,' describing the intensity of the woman's sorrow.
  • 垂涕 - Means 'shedding tears,' a common way to describe crying.
  • 夫夫也 - Colloquial expression meaning 'it's my husband,' used here to indicate possession.
  • 路不行而顾我 - Means 'he didn't walk on the road and looked at me,' implying a sense of abandonment or neglect.
  • 我以旷野无人，而子哭之恸 - Means 'I thought the wilderness was empty, and you were crying bitterly,' expressing the narrator's initial misunderstanding.
  • 夫死无路 - Means 'my husband died and left no way,' indicating a sense of loss and despair.
  • 实怆于心 - Means 'I was truly moved by your heartache,' showing empathy and sympathy.
  • 复择良匹 - Means 'to choose a good match again,' suggesting the possibility of finding a new partner.
  • 渺此一身 - Means 'alone and lost,' emphasizing the woman's isolation.
  • 其何能择 - Means 'how could I possibly choose,' expressing a sense of hopelessness.
  • 媵之可也 - Means 'to accompany him as a concubine,' indicating a willingness to accept a lesser role.
  • 忻然自荐 - Means 'gladly offered himself,' showing the man's eagerness.
  • 无庸 - Means 'no need,' indicating that the man's offer was unnecessary.
  • 飘若仙奔 - Means 'flew like a fairy,' describing the woman's swift and graceful movement.
  • 操井臼甚勤 - Means 'worked diligently at household chores,' indicating the woman's industriousness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金陵女子 - Refers to a woman from Jinling (modern-day Nanjing), a city with a rich cultural and historical background.
  • 沂水居民赵某 - Refers to a resident of Yishui named Zhao, indicating a specific location and person.
  • 白衣哭路侧 - Describes the woman in white mourning attire, which was a traditional sign of grief in ancient China.
  • 路不行而顾我 - Implies a sense of abandonment, which is a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 复择良匹 - Suggests the possibility of remarriage, which was a common practice in ancient Chinese society.
  • 媵之可也 - Indicates a willingness to accept a lesser role, reflecting the social norms of the time.
  • 飘若仙奔 - Describes the woman's movement in a poetic and metaphorical way, reflecting the literary style of the period.
  • 操井臼甚勤 - Indicates the woman's industriousness, which was highly valued in ancient Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 金陵 - The name of a city, modern-day Nanjing.
  • 沂水 - The name of a place, Yishui.
  • 赵某 - The name of a person, Zhao.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 積二年餘 - Accumulated over two years or after two years or more, indicating a period of time.
  • 猥相從 - Humbly followed you or graciously followed you, a modest expression used to describe following someone.
  • 忽已三年 - Suddenly it has been three years, indicating the passage of time without clear marking.
  • 今宜且去 - Now it is appropriate to part ways for now, a polite way of saying it is time to leave.
  • 曩言無家 - Previously said I had no family or earlier said I had no home, referring to a past statement.
  • 身父貨藥金陵 - My father sells medicine in Jinling, with 身父 meaning my father or my own father.
  • 可載藥往，可助資斧 - You can bring medicine and I will help with expenses, a polite offer of assistance.
  • 經營，為贳舆马 - To manage [this], I borrowed a carriage and horse, with 经营 meaning to manage or to make arrangements.
  • 辭之，出门徑去 - She declined, and went out straightaway, indicating she refused and left immediately.
  • 追之不及 - Could not catch up to her, a common expression for being too late to stop someone.
  • 瞬息遂杳 - In an instant, she vanished, indicating a sudden disappearance.
  • 居久之 - After a long time, indicating a period of time has passed.
  • 颇涉怀想 - Felt a bit of longing, expressing emotional yearning.
  • 市药诣金陵 - Bought medicine and went to Jinling, with 诣 meaning to go to or to visit.
  • 寄货旅邸 - Stored the goods at the inn, indicating temporary storage.
  • 訪諸衢市 - Visited the marketplaces, with 衢市 referring to crossroads or marketplaces.
  • 婿至矣 - The groom has arrived, a formal way of announcing someone's arrival.
  • 延之入 - Invited him in, a polite action.
  • 浣裳庭中 - Washing clothes in the courtyard, a domestic activity.
  • 不言亦不笑 - Spoke not, nor smiled, indicating emotional distance.
  • 浣不辍 - Did not stop washing, emphasizing continued action.
  • 衔恨遽出 - Left in haste, bearing resentment, indicating emotional conflict.
  • 曳之返 - Pulled him back, a physical act of bringing someone back.
  • 不顾如初 - Ignored him as before, indicating unchanged behavior.
  • 治具作饭 - Prepared food, a common expression for cooking.
  • 谋厚赠之 - Planned to give him a generous gift, indicating intention to reward.
  • 渠福薄 - His fortune is thin, meaning he is not destined for great things.
  • 多将不任 - Too much will not be bearable, indicating that too much would be too much for him.
  • 宜少慰其苦辛 - It is better to slightly comfort his hardships, suggesting a moderate approach.
  • 再检十数医方与之 - To give him another dozen or so medical prescriptions, indicating a generous offering of knowledge.
  • 便吃著不尽矣 - Then he will never run out of them, indicating an abundance of resources.
  • 已售之矣，直在此 - Already sold them, the money is here, indicating the money from the sale.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金陵 - The classical name for Nanjing, an ancient capital of China and a major cultural and commercial center.
  • 货药 - Refers to the selling of medicine, a common trade in ancient China, often associated with itinerant merchants or apothecaries.
  • 贳舆马 - Borrowed a carriage and horse, indicating a form of rental or borrowing in classical Chinese society.
  • 婿 - Refers to a husband or groom, indicating the relationship between the speaker and the woman.
  • 浣裳 - Washing clothes is a domestic activity, common for women in classical Chinese society.
  • 医方 - Medical prescriptions or formulas, referring to traditional Chinese medicine, which was a major field of study and practice.
  • 资斧 - A classical term for money or funds, often used in the context of travel or expenses.
  • 旅邸 - A traveler’s inn or temporary lodging, common for those traveling in ancient times.

Proper Nouns

  • 赵 - A personal name, likely ZHAO, a common Chinese surname.
  • 金陵 - A proper noun referring to Jinling, the classical name for Nanjing.
  • 舆马 - A technical term referring to carriage and horse, used in the context of travel or transport.
  • 资斧 - A technical term referring to money or funds, often used in the context of travel or expenses.
  • 旅邸 - A technical term referring to traveler’s inn or temporary lodging.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有奇验 - Means 'has extraordinary efficacy' or 'is remarkably effective.' Emphasizes the unusual and powerful nature of the remedy.
  • 沂水尚有能知其方者 - Translates to 'There are still people in Yishui who know the formula.' Indicates the persistence of knowledge about the remedy in a specific location.
  • 以蒜白接茅檐雨水，洗瘊赘 - Describes a specific method of using garlic stalks and rainwater from the eaves to wash away warts or blemishes. It is a detailed and somewhat unusual remedy.
  • 其方之一也，良效 - Means 'it is one of the formulas, and it is very effective.' Confirms the effectiveness of the remedy.
  • 抱病弥留 - Means 'sick and on the verge of death.' Describes a critical state of illness.
  • 忽觉下部热气渐升而上 - Translates to 'suddenly felt heat rising from below upwards.' Describes a sudden and unusual sensation.
  • 至股则足死，至腹则股又死，至心，心之死最难 - Means 'when it reaches the thighs, the feet die; when it reaches the abdomen, the thighs die; when it reaches the heart, the heart's death is the hardest.' Describes a progressive and intense process of dying.
  • 凡自童稚以及琐屑久忘之事 - Means 'all from childhood to trivial forgotten matters.' Indicates a wide range of memories and experiences.
  • 都随心血来，一潮过 - Translates to 'all come with the heartbeat, one wave after another.' Describes the recollection of memories in waves.
  • 如一善则心中清净宁帖 - Means 'if it is a good deed, the heart feels calm and peaceful.' Describes the positive emotional response to good deeds.
  • 一恶则懊憹烦燥 - Translates to 'if it is a bad deed, the heart feels restless and agitated.' Describes the negative emotional response to bad deeds.
  • 似油沸鼎中，其难堪之状，口不能肖似之 - Means 'like oil boiling in a pot, the unbearable state cannot be described in words.' Uses a vivid metaphor to describe an intense and uncomfortable feeling.
  • 直待平生所为，一一潮尽 - Translates to 'until all the deeds of a lifetime have passed through.' Indicates the completion of the process of recalling past actions.
  • 乃觉热气缕缕然，穿喉入脑自顶颠出 - Means 'then felt the heat rising in threads, passing through the throat and entering the brain, emerging from the top of the head.' Describes a final and intense sensation.
  • 腾上如炊，逾数十刻期，魂乃离窍忘躯壳矣 - Translates to 'rising like cooking, after dozens of time periods, the soul leaves the body and forgets the physical form.' Describes the final stage of the process, where the soul detaches from the body.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汤公 - Refers to a person named Tang Gong, likely a historical or literary figure. The context suggests he was a scholar or official.
  • 辛丑进士 - Refers to a person who passed the imperial examination in the year of Xin Chou (a Chinese zodiac year). This indicates a specific time period and social status.
  • 沂水 - Refers to Yishui, a place in China. The mention of this place adds a geographical and cultural context.
  • 蒜白接茅檐雨水 - This is a traditional Chinese remedy involving garlic and rainwater. It reflects the use of natural and local resources in traditional medicine.
  • 瘊赘 - Refers to warts or blemishes, a common medical condition in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 童稚 - Refers to childhood, emphasizing the early stages of life.
  • 琐屑久忘之事 - Refers to trivial and forgotten matters, indicating the breadth of human experience and memory.
  • 平生所为 - Refers to the deeds of a lifetime, emphasizing the totality of one's actions and experiences.
  • 魂乃离窍忘躯壳 - Refers to the soul leaving the body and forgetting the physical form, a concept found in traditional Chinese philosophy and religion.

Proper Nouns

  • 汤公 - The name of a person, likely a historical or literary figure.
  • 辛丑 - A Chinese zodiac year, indicating a specific time period.
  • 沂水 - A place in China, adding a geographical and cultural context.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 渺渺无归 - Describes a state of being lost and without direction.
  • 漂泊郊路间 - Means 'drifting along the outskirts of the road,' indicating a sense of aimlessness.
  • 高几盈寻 - Describes a giant's height, 'several spans tall,' where '寻' (xún) is an ancient unit of length.
  • 掇拾之纳诸袖中 - Means 'picking them up and putting them into the sleeve,' describing the action of collecting and storing something.
  • 叠肩压股，其人甚夥 - Describes a crowded situation, 'shoulders overlapping and pressing against thighs, with many people.'
  • 薅脑闷气，殆不可过 - Describes the discomfort caused by the crowding, 'pressing on the brain and suffocating the breath, almost unbearable.'
  • 公顿思惟佛能解厄 - Means 'the gentleman suddenly thought that the Buddha could resolve the crisis.'
  • 因宣佛号，才三四声 - Means 'he then recited the Buddha's name, only a few times.'
  • 飘堕袖外 - Means 'flying out of the sleeve,' indicating that the objects were ejected.
  • 巨人复纳之，三纳三堕 - Describes the repeated attempt and failure of the giant to put the objects back into his sleeve.
  • 公独立彷徨，未知何往之善 - Means 'the gentleman stood alone, hesitating, not knowing which way to go.'
  • 忆佛在西土，乃遂西 - Means 'remembering that the Buddha is in the Western Land, he then went west.'
  • 无何，见路侧一僧趺坐 - Means 'soon, he saw a monk sitting cross-legged by the roadside.'
  • 凡士子生死录，文昌及孔圣司之 - Means 'the records of scholars' births and deaths are managed by the God of Literature and Confucius.'
  • 必两处销名，乃可他适 - Means 'one must have their name removed from both places before they can go elsewhere.'
  • 宣圣南面坐 - Means 'the Holy Confucius sat facing south,' indicating a position of authority.
  • 伏祝之 - Means 'he bowed and prayed to him.'
  • 帝君检名曰 - Means 'the Lord of Heaven checked the name and said.'
  • 汝心诚正，宜复有生理 - Means 'your heart is sincere and upright, so you should have a chance at life again.'
  • 皮囊腐矣，非菩萨莫能为力 - Means 'your body is rotten, only the Bodhisattva can help.'
  • 见一殿阁如王者居 - Means 'he saw a palace like that of a king.'
  • 俯身入，果有神人，如世所传帝君像 - Means 'he bent down to enter, and indeed there was a divine being, resembling the image of the Lord of Heaven.'
  • 螺髻庄严，金容满月 - Describes the appearance of the divine being, 'with a coiled hairdo, majestic, golden face like a full moon.'
  • 瓶浸杨柳，翠碧垂烟 - Describes the scene, 'a bottle with a willow branch, green and blue smoke hanging down.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 佛 - Refers to the Buddha, a central figure in Buddhism.
  • 西土 - Refers to the Western Land, a traditional location associated with the Buddha in Chinese Buddhist tradition.
  • 文昌 - The God of Literature, a deity in Chinese folklore who oversees literary success.
  • 孔圣 - Confucius, a revered philosopher and sage in Chinese culture.
  • 帝君 - The Lord of Heaven, a deity in Taoist tradition.
  • 菩萨 - A Bodhisattva, a being in Buddhist tradition who has attained enlightenment but delays entering Nirvana to help others.
  • 螺髻 - A coiled hairstyle, often associated with deities in Chinese art.
  • 金容满月 - Describes a golden face, often used to describe the appearance of deities.
  • 瓶浸杨柳 - A common motif in Chinese art, symbolizing purity and compassion.

Proper Nouns

  • 佛 - The Buddha.
  • 文昌 - The God of Literature.
  • 孔圣 - Confucius.
  • 帝君 - The Lord of Heaven.
  • 菩萨 - A Bodhisattva.
  • 螺髻 - A coiled hairstyle, often associated with deities.
  • 金容满月 - Describes a golden face, often used to describe the appearance of deities.
  • 瓶浸杨柳 - A common motif in Chinese art, symbolizing purity and compassion.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公肅然稽首 - Formal gesture of deep respect and obeisance; the official respectfully bowed.
  • 拜述帝君言 - Bowed and recounted the words of the Heavenly Sovereign; a formal way of acknowledging and repeating divine instructions.
  • 菩薩難之 - The Bodhisattva challenged him; '難' can mean both challenge and test.
  • 公哀禱不已 - The official continued to pray with deep sorrow; indicating persistent and heartfelt supplication.
  • 尊者白言 - The venerable one spoke; referring to a respected figure offering advice or information.
  • 撮土可以為肉，折柳可以為骨 - A pinch of earth can become flesh, a willow branch can become bone; a magical transformation.
  • 手斷柳枝，傾瓶中水，合淨土為泥 - With a hand, he broke off a willow branch, poured water from the bottle, and mixed clean soil into mud; describing the magical process.
  • 拍附公體 - Applied the mixture to the official's body; indicating the application of the magical substance.
  • 童子携送灵所 - A boy carried it to the spirit's abode; referring to the place where spirits reside.
  • 推而合之 - Pushed it and closed it; likely referring to closing the coffin.
  • 棺中呻動，霍然病已 - In the coffin, there was groaning and movement, and suddenly the illness was cured; indicating the miraculous recovery.
  • 家人骇然集，扶而出之 - The family gathered in shock and helped him out; showing the surprise and relief of the family.
  • 计气绝已断七矣 - It was calculated that he had been dead for seven days; indicating the duration of his apparent death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阎罗 - Yama, the King of Hell in Buddhist cosmology, responsible for judging the dead.
  • 莱芜 - Laiwu, a city in Shandong Province, China.
  • 秀才 - Scholar, a title given to those who passed the lowest level of the imperial examinations.
  • 性直谅不阿 - Describes a person who is honest and straightforward, not fawning or flattering.
  • 数日辄死去 - Indicates a recurring pattern of death, possibly due to spiritual or supernatural reasons.
  • 僵然如尸 - Describes a state of death-like rigidity.
  • 三四日始醒 - Indicates the duration of the death-like state.
  • 隐秘不泄 - Refers to keeping secrets, often associated with spiritual or mystical experiences.
  • 张生者 - Another scholar, possibly a contemporary or acquaintance of Li Zhongzhi.
  • 阎罗也，余至阴司亦其属曹 - Indicates their roles in the underworld, with Li Zhongzhi as Yama and Zhang as his subordinate.
  • 门殿对联 - Refers to inscriptions on the entrance of a place, often containing moral or philosophical messages.
  • 提勘曹操，笞二十 - Refers to a specific case involving Cao Cao, a historical figure, and a legal punishment.
  • 畜道、剑山 - Refers to places in the underworld, with '畜道' being the path of animals and '剑山' being the mountain of swords.
  • 种种具在，宜得何罪 - Refers to the presence of various elements in the underworld and the question of appropriate punishment.
  • 不劳挹取 - Indicates that the answer is obvious, no need to seek further.
  • 数千年不决，何也 - Questions the unresolved nature of a case over thousands of years.
  • 岂以临刑之囚，快于速割，故使之求死不得也 - Suggests a possible reason for the unresolved nature, involving the prisoner's eagerness for quick death.
  • 异已 - Expresses surprise or disbelief.
  • 连琐 - A proper name, possibly a person or place.

Proper Nouns

  • 阎罗 - Yama, the King of Hell; rendered as Yama.
  • 莱芜 - City in Shandong Province, China; rendered as Laiwu.
  • 秀才 - Title for scholars; rendered as Scholar.
  • 张生者 - Mr. Zhang, a fellow scholar; rendered as Mr. Zhang.
  • 连琐 - Proper name; rendered as Lian Suo.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 与杨略话 - Chatting with Yang Lüe, where '与' (yǔ) means 'with' or 'together with,' and '略话' (lüè huà) implies a casual conversation.

  • 乃出，分手去 - Then he left, and they parted ways. '乃' (nǎi) means then, '出' (chū) means to leave, '分手' (fēn shǒu) means to part ways, and '去' (qù) means to go away.
  • 杨亦自归 - Yang also returned home. '亦' (yì) means also, '自' (zì) means himself/herself, and '归' (guī) means to return.
  • 越墙而仆 - He jumped over the wall and fell down. '越' (yuè) means to jump over, '墙' (qiáng) means wall, '而' (ér) is a conjunction meaning and, and '仆' (pú) means to fall down.
  • 惊寤 - Awoke in shock. '惊' (jīng) means shocked or startled, and '寤' (wù) means to wake up.
  • 听村鸡已乱鸣矣 - He heard the village chickens crowing chaotically. '听' (tīng) means to hear, '村鸡' (cūn jī) means village chickens, '已' (yǐ) means already, '乱' (luàn) means chaotic, and '鸣' (míng) means to crow.
  • 觉腕中痛甚 - He felt severe pain in his wrist. '觉' (jué) means to feel, '腕' (wàn) means wrist, '中' (zhōng) means in, '痛' (tòng) means pain, and '甚' (shèn) means severe.
  • 晓而视之 - In the morning he looked at it. '晓' (xiǎo) means morning, '而' (ér) is a conjunction meaning and, and '视之' (shì zhī) means to look at it.
  • 皮肉赤肿 - The skin and flesh were red and swollen. '皮肉' (pí ròu) means skin and flesh, '赤' (chì) means red, and '肿' (zhǒng) means swollen.
  • 亭午王生来 - At noon Wang came. '亭午' (tíng wǔ) means noon, '王' (wáng) is a proper name, and '生' (shēng) means person or individual.
  • 便言夜梦之奇 - He immediately said about the strange dream he had at night. '便' (biàn) means immediately, '言' (yán) means to say, '夜梦' (yè mèng) means night dream, '之' (zhī) is a particle, and '奇' (qí) means strange or unusual.
  • 杨曰：“未梦射否？” - Yang asked, 'Did you not dream about shooting?' '曰' (yuē) means said, '未' (wèi) means not, '梦射' (mèng shè) means dream about shooting, and '否' (fǒu) is a particle meaning not.
  • 王怪其先知 - Wang was surprised that Yang knew beforehand. '怪' (guài) means to be surprised, '其' (qí) means his/her, and '先知' (xiān zhī) means to know beforehand.
  • 杨出手示之，且告以故 - Yang showed his hand to him and also told him the reason. '出手' (chū shǒu) means to show one's hand, '示之' (shì zhī) means to show it to him/her, '且' (qiě) is a conjunction meaning also, '告' (gào) means to tell, and '以故' (yǐ gù) means for the reason.
  • 王忆梦中颜色，恨不真见 - Wang remembered the appearance in his dream and regretted not seeing it truly. '忆' (yì) means to remember, '梦中' (mèng zhōng) means in the dream, '颜色' (yán sè) means appearance or color, '恨' (hèn) means to regret, and '不真见' (bù zhēn jiàn) means not to see truly.
  • 自幸有功于女 - He was glad to have done something good for the girl. '自' (zì) means himself/herself, '幸' (xìng) means to be glad, '有功' (yǒu gōng) means to have done something good, and '于女' (yú nǚ) means for the girl.
  • 复请先容 - He again requested an introduction. '复' (fù) means again, '请' (qǐng) means to request, and '先容' (xiān róng) means to introduce someone.
  • 夜间，女来称谢 - That night, the girl came to express her gratitude. '夜间' (yè jiān) means at night, '女' (nǚ) means the girl, '来' (lái) means to come, and '称谢' (chēng xiè) means to express gratitude.
  • 杨归功王生 - Yang credited Wang with the success. '归功' (guī gōng) means to credit, and '王生' (wáng shēng) is a respectful way to address Wang.
  • 遂达诚恳 - And conveyed his sincerity. '遂' (suì) means and then, '达' (dá) means to convey, and '诚恳' (chéng kěn) means sincerity.

  • 彼爱妾佩刀 - He loves the knife I wear. '彼' (bǐ) means he, '爱' (ài) means to love, '妾' (qiè) means I (humble), '佩刀' (pèi dāo) means the knife I wear.

'刀' (dāo) means the knife, '实' (shí) means actually, '妾父' (qiè fù) means my father, '出使' (chū shǐ) means to go on a mission, '粤中' (yuè zhōng) means to Yue, '百金' (bǎi jīn) means a hundred gold coins, and '购之' (gòu zhī) means to purchase it.
  • 妾爱而有之，缠以金丝，瓣以明珠 - I loved it and kept it, wrapping it with gold thread and decorating it with pearls. '爱而有之' (ài ér yǒu zhī) means to love and keep it, '缠以金丝' (chán yǐ jīn sī) means to wrap it with gold thread, and '瓣以明珠' (bàn yǐ míng zhū) means to decorate it with pearls.
  • 大人怜妾夭亡，用以殉葬 - My parents pitied me for dying young and used it for my burial. '大人' (dà rén) means my parents, '怜' (lián) means to pity, '妾夭亡' (qiè yāo wáng) means I died young, and '用以殉葬' (yòng yǐ xùn zàng) means used it for my burial.

  • 时自棘中外窥 - Means 'sometimes he peeped out from the thorny bushes,' where '自棘中外窥' means 'peeped out from the thorny bushes.'
  • 见山中辄有夜叉往还 - Means 'he saw that there were demons coming and going in the mountains,' where '辄有夜叉往还' means 'there were demons coming and going.'
  • 大惧，不敢少动 - Means 'he was greatly afraid and dared not move even a little,' where '大惧' means 'greatly afraid' and '不敢少动' means 'dared not move even a little.'
  • 一日北风策策 - Means 'one day the north wind blew strongly,' where '策策' means 'blew strongly.'
  • 少年忽至，引与急窜 - Means 'the youth suddenly arrived, led him and hurriedly fled,' where '引与急窜' means 'led him and hurriedly fled.'
  • 嘱曰：“所言勿忘却。” - Means 'he instructed, 'Do not forget what I said,' where '勿忘却' means 'do not forget.'
  • 又以肉置几上，商乃归 - Means 'he also placed some meat on the table, and the merchant then returned,' where '以肉置几上' means 'placed some meat on the table' and '商乃归' means 'the merchant then returned.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夜叉 - Refers to a demon or a mythical creature, often used in Chinese literature to describe supernatural beings.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a series of events in a compact form: a merchant sailing the sea, encountering a storm, being blown to Womai, just as he was about to disembark, seeing a youth, and being surprised.
  • This clause describes the merchant's interaction with the youth: knowing he was a Chinese person, he asked where he lived, and the merchant told him.
  • This clause describes the youth's actions: dragging the merchant into a small cave in a secluded valley, outside which were all thorny bushes, and instructing him not to leave.
  • This clause describes the youth's actions after a while: bringing deer meat to feed the merchant.
  • This clause describes the youth's statement: saying, 'My father is also a foreigner,' where '交人' is a classical expression for 'foreigner.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 走三四十里 - A phrase indicating the distance traveled on foot, roughly 'walked thirty to forty li.'

  • 囊物赴都 - Literally 'satchel goods to the capital,' meaning to carry goods to the capital city.
  • 要取之，略可千金 - 'To seize it, roughly worth a thousand gold coins.' '要取之' is a phrase meaning 'to seize or take it by force.'
  • 意气始得扬 - 'Only then did his spirit or pride rise.' '意气' refers to one's spirit, pride, or self-confidence.
  • 方疾骛间 - 'Just as he was rushing swiftly.' '疾骛' means to gallop or rush quickly.
  • 闻后有蹄声 - 'Hearing hoofbeats behind.' '蹄声' is the sound of hooves.
  • 回首则僮易跛骡来，驶若飞 - 'Looking back, he saw the boy on a lame donkey coming, galloping like a bird.' '驶若飞' is a metaphor for moving very fast.
  • 叱曰 - 'He shouted,' indicating a command or reprimand.
  • 男子勿行 - 'Do not go, man!' A direct command.
  • 猎取之货宜少瓜分 - 'The goods taken by hunting should be divided in small portions.' '瓜分' is a metaphor for dividing something among several people.
  • 邢以僮貌不扬，又无弓矢，易之 - 'Xing thought the boy was unimpressive in appearance and lacked a bow and arrow, so he underestimated him.' '易之' is a verb meaning 'to consider someone inferior or underestimate them.'
  • 一发三矢连遱不断，如群隼飞翔 - 'He shot three arrows in quick succession, unbroken, like a flock of hawks in flight.' '连遱不断' means 'continuously without interruption.'
  • 僮殊不忙迫 - 'The boy was not at all flustered or hurried.' '殊不忙迫' is a negated form meaning 'not at all hurried.'
  • 手接二，口衔一 - 'He caught two with his hands and held one in his mouth.' This is a vivid, almost superhuman description.
  • 乃翁偬遽，未暇寻得弓来，此物亦无用处，请即掷还 - 'Your master is in a hurry and hasn’t had time to find a bow; this thing is also of no use, please just throw it back.' '乃翁' is 'your master' or 'your father,' '偬遽' means 'in a hurry.'
  • 遂于指上脱铁镮，穿矢其中，以手力掷，呜呜风鸣 - 'He then took off an iron ring from his finger, threaded the arrow through it, and with his hand's strength, threw it, making a whistling sound through the wind.' '脱铁镮' is to remove an iron ring, '穿矢其中' is to thread the arrow through it.
  • 邢急拨以弓，弦适触铁镮，铿然断绝，弓亦绽裂 - 'Xing hastily drew his bow, but the string struck the iron ring and with a metallic clang, snapped, and the bow split.' '拨以弓' is to draw the bow, '弦适触铁镮' is the string just happened to strike the iron ring.
  • 邢惊绝，未及觑避，矢过贯耳，不觉翻坠 - 'Xing was stunned, hadn’t even had time to look away, when the arrow passed through his ear and he fell over without realizing it.' '惊绝' is a strong expression for being completely shocked.
  • 僮下骑便将搜括 - 'The boy dismounted and immediately began to search and seize.' '搜括' is a term for searching and taking.
  • 邢以弓卧挞之 - 'Xing tried to strike him with the bow while lying down.' '卧挞' is to strike while lying down.
  • 僮夺弓去，拗折为两，又折为四，抛置之 - 'The boy took the bow, bent it in two, then broke it into four parts, and threw it away.' '拗折' is to bend and break.
  • 已，乃一手握邢两臂，一足踏邢两股，臂若缚，股若压，极力不能少动 - 'Then, with one hand he held both of Xing’s arms, and with one foot he stepped on both of Xing’s thighs; his arms were like bound, his thighs like pressed—Xing could not move at all, no matter how hard he tried.' '股若压' is 'thighs like pressed.'
  • 腰中束带双叠可骈三指许 - 'The belt around his waist was doubled over and could be compared to the width of three fingers.' '骈三指许' is 'approximately the width of three fingers.'
  • 僮以一手捏之，随手断如灰烬 - 'The boy took one hand and crushed it, and it broke like ashes.' '随手断如灰烬' is a vivid metaphor for something breaking easily.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 異史氏曰 - Conventional formula used in classical Chinese narrative texts, akin to 'The historian says' or 'The compiler remarks,' indicating a commentary or moral reflection by the author.
  • 一笑之知，許之以身 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'knowing from a single smile, and offering one’s life in return,' suggesting a romantic or emotional bond based on a fleeting moment.
  • 世人或議其痴 - 'The world may call it foolish,' where '議其痴' is a euphemistic way of saying people criticize or mock the action as foolish or imprudent.
  • 此知希之貴 - 'This is the value of rare knowledge or understanding,' where '知希' refers to rare or precious knowledge.
  • 賢豪所以感結而不能自已也 - 'This is why the virtuous and heroic are moved and cannot help but act,' indicating emotional or moral compulsion.
  • 顧茫茫海内 - 'But throughout the vast world,' a phrase used to contrast the ideal with the reality.
  • 锦绣才人 - 'Fine-talented men,' referring to men of literary or artistic talent.
  • 僅傾心于峨眉之一笑也 - 'Only devoting their hearts to the smile of a woman (Eimei),' where '峨眉' is a classical term for a beautiful woman or a reference to the mountain, often used metaphorically for beauty.
  • 悲夫 - Exclamatory expression meaning 'Alas!' or 'How sad!'
  • 與嚴生少相狎，长相谑也 - 'Were close companions in youth and often teased each other,' where '狎' means to be familiar or intimate, and '谑' means to jest or tease.
  • 口给交御 - 'Quick-witted and skilled in verbal exchange,' where '口给' means eloquent or quick of tongue, and '交御' means to engage in mutual verbal sparring.
  • 惟恐不工 - 'Afraid that it would not be skillful enough,' indicating a desire to outdo one another in wit.
  • 窥严将至 - 'Spying that Yan was about to arrive,' where '窥' means to spy or watch, and '将至' means about to arrive.
  • 窃语云 - 'Secretly said,' where '窃语' is a euphemism for whispering or speaking in secret.
  • 某妻与我最昵 - 'His wife is closest to me,' where '昵' means to be intimate or close.
  • 众不信 - 'The others did not believe,' referring to the group of people who overheard the remark.
  • 霍因捏造端末 - 'Huo fabricated the beginning and end,' meaning he invented the story from start to finish.
  • 如不信，其阴侧有双疣 - 'If you don’t believe, her private parts have two warts,' a direct and crude statement used to prove the claim.
  • 严止窗外，听之既悉，不入径去 - 'Yan stopped at the window, listened to everything, and then left without entering,' indicating he heard the conversation and decided not to confront them.
  • 至家苦掠其妻 - 'Upon returning home, he cruelly beat his wife,' where '苦掠' means to beat or torment harshly.
  • 妻不服，搒益残 - 'His wife did not confess, so he beat her even more cruelly,' where '搒' is a form of corporal punishment.
  • 妻不堪虐，自经死 - 'His wife could not endure the cruelty and hanged herself,' where '自经' is a euphemism for suicide by hanging.
  • 霍始大悔 - 'Huo began to deeply regret,' indicating a sudden realization of his wrongdoing.
  • 然亦不敢向严而白其诬矣 - 'But he also dared not go to Yan and clear his name,' where '白其诬' means to prove that the accusation was false.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 異史氏曰 - This is a classical narrative device found in *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* (*Liaozhai Zhiyi*), a famous collection of supernatural and moral tales by Pu Songling. The term '異史氏' is a pseudonym used by the author to comment on the story, similar to a historian or moral commentator.
  • 田横五百人 - Refers to the legendary group of 500 loyal followers of Tianheng (田横), a Han dynasty general who resisted the Han dynasty and committed suicide when his followers were executed. This allusion is used to contrast the foolishness of the world with the loyalty and honor of historical figures.
  • 峨眉 - A classical reference to Meishan (Mount Emei), one of the Four Sacred Mountains of Chinese Buddhism, or a metaphor for a beautiful woman, as in the phrase '峨眉淡扫' (a light stroke of the eyebrows of Emei).
  • 文登霍生 - Wendeng is a historical county in present-day Shandong Province. Huo Sheng (霍生) is a fictional character, likely a scholar or literatus.
  • 严生 - Yan Sheng (严生), another fictional character, likely a rival or friend of Huo Sheng.
  • 自经死 - A euphemistic expression for suicide by hanging, common in classical Chinese literature to avoid explicit descriptions of death.
  • 搒 - A form of corporal punishment, often involving beating or flogging, used in ancient Chinese legal systems.
  • 白其诬 - To expose or prove that an accusation is false, a common legal or moral concern in classical Chinese texts.

Proper Nouns

  • 异史氏 - A pseudonym used by the author of *Liaozhai Zhiyi* to introduce commentary or moral reflection.
  • 田横 - A historical figure from the Warring States period and early Han dynasty, known for his loyalty and the tragic fate of his followers.
  • 文登 - A historical county in modern-day Shandong Province, China.
  • 霍生 - A fictional character, likely a scholar or literatus from Wendeng.
  • 严生 - Another fictional character, likely a rival or close associate of Huo Sheng.
  • 峨眉 - A classical reference to Mount Emei or a metaphor for a beautiful woman.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 异史氏曰 - Common phrase in classical Chinese literature, meaning 'The historian of strange tales says,' used to introduce the author's commentary or moral reflection.
  • 家有女豫让而不知 - Literally, 'If one has a daughter like Yu Rang and does not know it,' where 'Yu Rang' is a metaphor for a virtuous and capable woman. The phrase emphasizes the ignorance or neglect of such a valuable person.
  • 则兄之为丈夫者可知矣 - Rhetorical statement meaning 'then one can see what kind of man the brother is,' implying that the brother's character is revealed by his failure to recognize the daughter's worth.
  • 萧萧易水 - Refers to the story of Jing Ke, who attempted to assassinate the King of Qin near the Yishui River. The phrase evokes a sense of heroic sacrifice and honor.
  • 亦将羞而不流 - Literally, 'would also be ashamed and not flow,' meaning that even the Yishui River would be embarrassed by the cowardice of the people.
  • 况碌碌与世浮沉者耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how much more so for those who are mediocre and drift with the world?' emphasizing the moral failing of ordinary people.
  • 愿天下闺中人 - Means 'I wish for all women in the world,' expressing a broad wish for all women.
  • 买丝绣之 - Means 'to buy silk and embroider it,' referring to the act of creating a memorial or tribute.
  • 其功德当不减于奉壮缪也 - Means 'their virtue and merit will not be less than those who serve the great and brave,' suggesting that the act of honoring the woman will be as virtuous as serving a great hero.
  • 夜俟母寝 - Means 'waited until his mother was asleep,' indicating the secretive nature of the action.
  • 潜持铁槌去眠父所 - Means 'secretly took an iron mallet and went to sleep where his father had been,' describing the son's preparation for revenge.
  • 冀报父仇 - Means 'hoping to avenge his father's death,' expressing the son's motivation.
  • 少间一狼来逡巡嗅之 - Means 'after a while, a wolf came cautiously and sniffed at him,' describing the wolf's initial approach.
  • 又渐俯首舐其股 - Means 'then gradually lowered its head to lick his thigh,' indicating the wolf's attempt to befriend the son.
  • 江迄不动 - Means 'Yu Jiang remained motionless,' highlighting the son's resolve.
  • 既而欢跃直前 - Means 'then happily and boldly advanced,' describing the wolf's final attack.
  • 将龁其领 - Means 'about to bite his neck,' indicating the wolf's intent to kill.
  • 江急以锤击狼脑，立毙 - Means 'Yu Jiang quickly struck the wolf's head with the hammer, killing it instantly,' describing the son's decisive action.
  • 起置草中 - Means 'stood up and placed it in the grass,' indicating the son's disposal of the first wolf.
  • 少间又一狼来如前状 - Means 'after a while, another wolf came in the same manner,' repeating the pattern of the first wolf.
  • 以至中夜杳无至者 - Means 'until midnight, no more wolves came,' indicating the end of the wolves' attacks.

  • 萧萧易水 - Refers to the Yishui River, where Jing Ke attempted to assassinate the King of Qin. The phrase is used to evoke a sense of heroic sacrifice and honor.
  • 壮缪 - Refers to Liu Bei, the founder of the Shu Han state during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his bravery and loyalty.

  • 豺狼之行本合尽诛，但吾所不忍，姑释去，再犯不宥 - Describes a conditional statement: 'The actions of wolves and tigers should be completely exterminated, but I cannot bear to do so, I will let you go for now, but if you offend again, I will not forgive you.'

  • 于是里中人渐知为白莲教戚裔 - Describes a gradual realization: 'The villagers gradually realized he was a descendant of the White Lotus sect.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 货殖之来也苟，固宜有散亡 - This phrase is elliptical and requires careful interpretation to convey the idea that wealth comes easily but is also fleeting.
  • 蛇蝎之乡不可久居 - This metaphorical expression needs to be translated in a way that captures its figurative meaning.
  • 因贱售其业而去之，止于益都之西鄙 - This sentence describes a sequence of actions and requires clear separation to maintain logical flow.
  • 女为人灵巧，善居积，经纪过于男子 - This sentence lists multiple attributes and requires careful translation to preserve the parallel structure.
  • 尝开琉璃厂，每进工人而指点之 - This sentence describes a past action and the associated practice, requiring clear temporal markers.
  • 一切棋灯，其奇式幻采，诸肆莫能及 - This sentence highlights the uniqueness of the products and requires careful translation to convey the quality and uniqueness.
  • 以故直昂得速售 - This sentence explains the reason for the high price and quick sales, requiring clear causal markers.
  • 居数年财益称雄 - This sentence describes the outcome after several years, requiring clear temporal and causal markers.
  • 而女督课婢仆严，食指数百无冗口 - This sentence describes the woman's management of her household, requiring clear subject-verb relationships.
  • 暇辄与丁烹茗着棋，或观书史为乐 - This sentence describes her leisure activities, requiring clear separation of the different activities.
  • 钱谷出入以及婢仆业，凡五日一课，妇自持筹，丁为之点籍唱名数焉 - This sentence describes the financial management process, requiring clear separation of the different actions and responsibilities.
  • 勤者赏赍有差，惰者鞭挞罚膝立 - This sentence describes the reward and punishment system, requiring clear separation of the different outcomes.

  • 顷刻间数巨万矣 - A phrase meaning 'in a moment, several million,' with '顷刻间' meaning 'in a moment' or 'in a short time,' and '数巨万' meaning 'several million.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 白镪 - Another term for silver, often used in classical texts to denote precious metal.
  • 乡选 - Refers to the provincial examination, a part of the imperial examination system used to select officials in ancient China.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '便为盥沐，颜色光泽，眉目焕映，母子俱喜' describes a series of actions and their effects, with '便为盥沐' initiating the sequence, followed by descriptions of the mother and son's appearance and happiness.
  • The clause '然家三口，日仅一啖，母泣曰：“吾母子固应尔；所怜者，负吾贤妇！”' introduces a contrast between the family's current situation and their past, with '然' indicating a shift in tone.
  • The clause '女笑慰之曰：“新妇在乞人中，稔其况味，今日视之，觉有天堂地狱之别。”' presents the daughter's response, with '笑慰之' meaning 'to comfort with a smile.'
  • The phrase '母为解颐' indicates the mother's relief or amusement, with '解颐' meaning 'to smile' or 'to be relieved.'
  • The long sequence '女一日入闲舍中，见断草丛丛无隙地，渐入内室，尘埃积中，暗陬有物堆积，蹴之迕足，拾视皆朱提' describes the daughter's discovery of gold, with '渐入内室' meaning 'gradually entering the inner room.'
  • The sequence '急赎归，断砖残缺，所藏石子俨然露焉，颇觉失望，及发他砖，则灿灿皆白镪也' describes the process of uncovering the gold, with '急赎归' indicating urgency.
  • The phrase '顷刻间数巨万矣' emphasizes the sudden and significant change in the family's wealth.
  • The clause '由是赎田产，市奴仆，门庭华好过昔日' summarizes the family's newfound prosperity.
  • The phrase '若不自立，负我宫叔' expresses the daughter's determination to succeed.
  • The clause '刻志下帷，三年中乡选' describes the daughter's dedication to her studies and her goal of passing the provincial examination.

  • 蓋吕祖讳也 - Means 'it is the taboo name of Lü Zu,' where '讳' (hui) refers to a taboo name or name that should not be spoken.
  • 谕鬼 - Means 'to admonish the ghosts,' indicating a command or warning to supernatural beings.

  • 谕鬼 - Indicates a command or warning to supernatural beings, reflecting the power of the living over the dead.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 何來 - Means 'where are you coming from?'

  • 俄女子自后来 - Means 'soon the woman came from behind.'

  • 仆伪作醒状 - Means 'the servant pretended to wake up.'
  • 问其为谁 - Means 'asked who she was.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 未肯暂脱，必其要害 - Means 'she was not willing to take it off temporarily, and it must be her vital part,' suggesting that the shirt was important to her.
  • 执此可以胁之 - Means 'holding onto this can threaten her,' indicating the servant's plan to use the shirt as leverage.
  • 夜来女至 - Means 'that night, the woman came,' describing the woman's arrival.
  • 及寝，阴掬其衫 - Means 'when they went to bed, he secretly took her shirt,' describing the servant's action.
  • 女急啼，力脱而去 - Means 'the woman cried out in distress and struggled to take it off and leave,' showing the woman's reaction.
  • 从此遂绝 - Means 'from then on, they never met again,' indicating the end of their relationship.
  • 后仆自他方归 - Means 'later, the servant returned from elsewhere,' describing the servant's return.
  • 遥见女子坐道周 - Means 'he saw the woman sitting by the roadside from afar,' describing the servant's encounter.
  • 至前则举袖障面 - Means 'when he approached, she raised her sleeve to cover her face,' showing the woman's gesture.
  • 仆下骑呼曰 - Means 'the servant dismounted and called out,' introducing the servant's action.
  • 何作此态 - Means 'why are you acting this way?' asking the woman about her behavior.
  • 女乃起握手曰 - Means 'the woman then stood up and took his hand, saying,' introducing the woman's response.
  • 我谓子已忘旧好矣 - Means 'I thought you had forgotten our old affection,' expressing the woman's surprise.
  • 既恋恋有故人意 - Means 'since you still have feelings for your old friend,' indicating the servant's lingering affection.
  • 情尚可原 - Means 'your feelings can still be excused,' showing the woman's understanding.
  • 前事出于主命，亦不汝怪也 - Means 'the previous matter was due to the master's command, and I do not blame you,' explaining the woman's forgiveness.
  • 但缘分已尽，今设小酌，请入为别 - Means 'but our缘分has ended, so I have prepared a small feast, please come in to say goodbye,' indicating the woman's decision to part ways.
  • 时秋初，高梁正茂 - Means 'it was early autumn, and the high粱was flourishing,' setting the scene.
  • 女携与俱入 - Means 'the woman led him inside,' describing the woman's action.
  • 则中有巨第 - Means 'there was a large mansion inside,' describing the setting.
  • 系马而入 - Means 'he tied his horse and went inside,' describing the servant's action.
  • 厅堂中酒肴已列 - Means 'the hall was already set with wine and dishes,' describing the feast.
  • 甫坐，群婢行炙 - Means 'as soon as they sat down, the maids began serving hot food,' describing the servants' actions.
  • 日将暮，仆有事欲覆主命，遂别 - Means 'as the day was ending, the servant had something to do and had to return to the master's command, so he left,' describing the servant's departure.
