Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初来未解装 - Colloquial expression meaning 'just arrived and not yet unpacked,' indicating the protagonist's initial state of disorientation or unfamiliarity.
  • 无所告诉 - Means 'no one to turn to' or 'no one to confide in,' expressing the protagonist's isolation.
  • 漫膝行而哀之 - A vivid description of the protagonist crawling on their knees and pleading, emphasizing their desperation.
  • 汝愈矣 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'you are better now,' used to indicate recovery or resolution.
  • 既欲得裘，何得复无礼 - Rhetorical question meaning 'if you want the fur coat, why must you be disrespectful?' used to chastise the protagonist.
  • 稽其时日，即取酒夜也 - Requires contextual interpretation: 'when checking the time, it was the night when the wine was taken,' suggesting a connection between the theft and the protagonist's illness.
  • 群惮其神 - Means 'the crowd fears his spirit,' indicating the protagonist's mysterious or supernatural power.
  • 以桑梓谊诣刘 - Means 'visited Liu out of hometown affection,' indicating a personal or familial connection.
  • 使不能欺，以实告 - Means 'the messenger could not deceive, and reported the truth,' showing the messenger's honesty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 仆 - A humble self-reference, common in Classical Chinese.
  • 刘 - Likely a personal name, possibly a historical or fictional figure.
  • 提学使 - A high-ranking official in charge of education and examination administration during the Ming and Qing dynasties.
  • 圣仙 - A term for a revered or divine being, often used in folklore or religious contexts.
  • 绘小像 - The act of painting a small portrait, a common practice in Chinese culture.
  • 额上得毋痛否 - Colloquial expression meaning 'does your forehead hurt?' used to inquire about discomfort.

Proper Nouns

  • 仆 - A humble self-reference.
  • 刘 - A personal name.
  • 张道一 - A proper name, possibly a historical or fictional figure.
  • 圣仙 - A term for a revered or divine being.
  • 额上得毋痛否 - A colloquial expression.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 无何 - Indicates a short period of time, common in classical Chinese to denote a brief interval.
  • 请觐之 - A formal and respectful way of asking to see someone, reflecting the hierarchical nature of ancient Chinese society.
  • 固辞之 - Reflects the cultural value of modesty and the importance of declining politely.
  • 请之坚 - Shows the persistence and determination of the suitor, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 非他人 - Implies that the suitor is special or unique, often used to highlight the significance of a person.
  • 拒之深 - Emphasizes the depth of the refusal, indicating strong resistance.
  • 必当有以赠之 - Reflects the cultural expectation of reciprocity and the importance of gifts in social interactions.
  • 望我奢 - Highlights the cultural value of generosity and the potential for high expectations in relationships.
  • 自度不能满其志 - Shows the self-awareness and humility of the speaker.
  • 故适不欲见耳 - Therefore, I don't want to see him at all, where '适' (shì) means 'therefore' and '不欲见' (bù yù jiàn) means 'do not want to see.'
  • 乃许以十日见 - Agreed to meet after ten days, where '乃' (nǎi) indicates a result and '许以' (xǔ yǐ) means 'agreed to.'
  • 隔帘揖之 - Greeted him through a screen, where '隔帘' (gé lián) means 'through a screen' and '揖' (yī) means 'to bow or greet.'
  • 少致存问 - Lightly expressed greetings, where '少' (shǎo) means 'lightly' and '致存问' (zhì cún wèn) means 'expressed greetings.'
  • 仪容隐约 - Appearance was vague or indistinct, where '仪容' (yí róng) means 'appearance' and '隐约' (yǐn yuē) means 'vague' or 'indistinct.'
  • 不敢审谛 - Dared not look closely, where '不敢' (bù gǎn) means 'dared not' and '审谛' (shěn dì) means 'look closely.'
  • 数步之外辄回眸注盼 - Every few steps, she would turn her eyes back to look, where '数步之外' (shǔ bù zhī wài) means 'a few steps away' and '辄回眸注盼' (zhé huí móu zhù pàn) means 'would turn her eyes back to look.'
  • 烈烈如鸮鸣 - Loud and harsh like an owl's cry, where '烈烈' (liè liè) means 'loud and harsh' and '鸮鸣' (xiāo míng) means 'owl's cry.'
  • 胫股皆软 - Legs and thighs became weak, where '胫股' (jìng gǔ) means 'legs and thighs' and '皆软' (jiē ruǎn) means 'all became weak.'
  • 摇摇然如丧魂魄 - Shook as if losing one's soul, where '摇摇然' (yáo yáo rán) means 'shook' and '如丧魂魄' (rú sàng hún pò) means 'as if losing one's soul.'
  • 坐移时始稍定 - Sat for a while before gradually calming down, where '坐移时' (zuò yí shí) means 'sat for a while' and '始稍定' (shǐ shāo dìng) means 'gradually calmed down.'
  • 适闻笑声，如听霹雳 - Just heard the laughter, it was like hearing thunder, where '适闻' (shì wén) means 'just heard' and '如听霹雳' (rú tīng pī lì) means 'like hearing thunder.'
  • 竟不觉身为己有 - Didn't even feel his body belonged to him, where '竟不觉' (jìng bù jué) means 'didn't even feel' and '身为己有' (shēn wéi jǐ yǒu) means 'body belonged to him.'
  • 圣仙日与丈人居 - The divine being spends days with my father-in-law, where '圣仙' (shèng xiān) means 'divine being' and '日与丈人居' (rì yǔ zhàng rén jū) means 'spends days with my father-in-law.'
  • 宁不知我素性挥霍 - How could she not know my usual extravagance, where '宁不知' (níng bù zhī) means 'how could she not know' and '素性挥霍' (sù xìng huī huò) means 'usual extravagance.'
  • 不惯使小钱 - Not used to using small amounts of money, where '不惯' (bù guàn) means 'not used to' and '使小钱' (shǐ xiǎo qián) means 'using small amounts of money.'
  • 囊底适罄 - My purse just emptied, where '囊底' (náng dǐ) means 'purse' and '适罄' (shì qìng) means 'just emptied.'
  • 向结伴至汴梁 - Recently went with companions to Bianliang, where '向' (xiàng) means 'recently' and '结伴至汴梁' (jié bàn zhì biàn liáng) means 'went with companions to Bianliang.'
  • 其城为河伯占据 - The city was occupied by the River God, where '其城' (qí chéng) means 'the city' and '为河伯占据' (wéi hé bó jù jū) means 'was occupied by the River God.'
  • 库藏皆没水中 - All the treasuries were submerged in water, where '库藏' (kù cáng) means 'treasuries' and '皆没水中' (jiē méi shuǐ zhōng) means 'were submerged in water.'
  • 入水各得些须 - Those who entered the water each got a little, where '入水' (rù shuǐ) means 'entered the water' and '各得些须' (gè dé xiē xū) means 'each got a little.'
  • 何能饱无餍之求 - How could they satisfy insatiable demands, where '何能' (hé néng) means 'how could' and '饱无餍之求' (bǎo wú yàn zhī qiú) means 'satisfy insatiable demands.'
  • 且我纵能厚馈 - Even if I could give generously, where '且' (qiě) means 'even if' and '我纵能厚馈' (wǒ zòng néng hòu kuì) means 'I could give generously.'
  • 彼福薄亦不能任 - He has thin fortune and cannot bear it, where '彼福薄' (bǐ fú báo) means 'he has thin fortune' and '亦不能任' (yì bù néng rèn) means 'cannot bear it.'
